serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of raw Ceylon spinach is approximately 85 g (about 1 cup of loosely packed leaves and tender stems), as commonly consumed in salads or as a raw vegetable side.

nutrient_source.title: Ceylon Spinach (Basella alba), raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Ceylon spinach, raw (F14010014). Values match input record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Low-Carbohydrate Leafy Vegetables — Analogous Estimate Based on University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Ceylon spinach (Basella alba) is not individually listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. A GI of 15 (low) is assigned by analogy to other raw leafy green vegetables (e.g., spinach, GI ~15) documented in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 85 g serving is 0.0 due to negligible available carbohydrate (0.0 g/100g per HPB). This estimate is clinically conservative and consistent with the food's negligible carbohydrate content.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (23 mg/100g) and high potassium (325 mg/100g) yielding a favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Ceylon spinach is strongly suitable for hypertensive patients: its very low sodium content (23 mg/100g) and high potassium (325 mg/100g) support blood pressure reduction in line with DASH diet principles; patients should avoid adding high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or oyster sauce during preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible available carbohydrate (0.0 g/100g) and high dietary fibre (3.4 g/100g) producing a glycaemic load of zero
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ceylon spinach is an excellent choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: its zero available carbohydrate content and high dietary fibre (3.4 g/100g) result in a glycaemic load of zero per serving, supporting stable postprandial blood glucose; it can be freely incorporated into low-carbohydrate meal plans as a nutrient-dense leafy green.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high potassium, and high dietary fibre supporting cardiovascular risk reduction
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ceylon spinach is highly suitable for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease: its negligible fat content (0.16 g/100g), zero cholesterol, high potassium (325 mg/100g), and high dietary fibre (3.4 g/100g) collectively support healthy lipid profiles and blood pressure management; regular consumption as part of a plant-rich diet is consistent with AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory micronutrient profile including calcium (37 mg/100g) and high dietary fibre supporting gut-immune axis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ceylon spinach may benefit rheumatoid arthritis patients through its anti-inflammatory micronutrient profile and high fibre content supporting gut microbiome health; however, patients with a history of oxalate-related kidney stones should moderate intake due to the oxalate content of Basella alba.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ceylon spinach (Basella alba), also known as Malabar spinach, Indian spinach, or vine spinach, is a tropical leafy green vegetable widely consumed across South Asia, Southeast Asia, and parts of Africa. In its raw form, it features thick, succulent, bright green leaves and tender stems with a mildly mucilaginous texture and a slightly tangy, mild flavour profile. Unlike true spinach (Spinacia oleracea), Ceylon spinach is a climbing vine that thrives in hot, humid climates, making it a staple leafy green in Singapore, Malaysia, India, and Sri Lanka. Raw Ceylon spinach is nutritionally notable for its very low caloric density (16 kcal/100g), negligible available carbohydrate (0.0 g/100g), meaningful protein content (1.98 g/100g), and high dietary fibre (3.4 g/100g). Its potassium content (325 mg/100g) is particularly significant for cardiovascular and blood pressure management, while its low sodium (23 mg/100g) makes it ideal for DASH-compliant diets. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), with a glycaemic load of zero per serving due to negligible carbohydrate content, making it an excellent vegetable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. Ceylon spinach is naturally vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. It is commonly stir-fried with garlic, blanched, or added to soups and curries in Asian cuisines. Its oxalate content is a consideration for individuals prone to kidney stones.

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14010014) with a confidence of 0.95. The food identity is unambiguous. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database comparators (raw leafy greens, GI ~15) and is clearly documented as such.
notes: carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14010014); Ceylon spinach in its raw form contains negligible available carbohydrate as reported by the source — naturally zero for this raw leafy vegetable. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB source; fat content (0.16 g/100g) is predominantly unsaturated. sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero as this is a plant-based food containing no dietary cholesterol — naturally zero. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and micronutrient data beyond those reported are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this food as of 2026-04-16. USDA FoodData Central entry for Basella alba (NDB 11026) may provide supplementary micronutrient data but was not used as primary source to preserve HPB provenance integrity. GI value of 15 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database comparators for raw leafy green vegetables; no direct GI measurement for Basella alba was identified in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. The input record referenced vitamin C at 102 mg/100g in the rheumatoid arthritis health suitability note; this value was not confirmed in the HPB Foodo database entry and has not been carried into per_100g to preserve source integrity — vitamin_c_mg is set to -1 pending verification.
